coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared I/II emission and absorption carbon dots via constructing localized excited/charge transfer state for multiphoton imaging and photothermal therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Wen-Bo
Chen, Dan-Dan
Liu, Kai-Kai
Wang, Yong
Zhou, Rui
Song, Shi-Yu
Li, Fu-Kui
Sui, Lai-Zhi
Lou, Qing
Hou, Lin
Shan, Chong-Xin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with absorption and emission properties in near-infrared (NIR) bio-windows highlight their application in the integration of diagnosis and treatment. In this work, CDs with NIR-I emission and NIR-II absorption have been demonstrated, by constructing localized excited state and charge-transfer state. NIR fluorescence together with photothermal effect was observed in the CDs under NIR light illumination. The electron donors (D) and acceptors (A) groups around the carbon core of the CDs lead to D- π -A charge-transfer state, producing a strong electronic push–pull effect and yielding the desired NIR-II absorption with a maximum absorption peak at 1033 nm. Localized excited state from large conjugated rigid sp2 domain leads to NIR-I emission of 670 nm. Multiphoton-induced NIR-I emission (quantum yield is 29%) and NIR-II induced heat with photothermal conversion efficiency of 41.19% have been realized. In addition, multiphoton cellular imaging and cancer photothermal therapy using the CDs as imaging and photothermal agents have been demonstrated. The results provide a route to CDs with NIR absorption/emission characters and prospect their potential application in multiple-photon bioimaging and photothermal therapy of cancers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Near-infrared absorption
Near-infrared emission
Multiphoton imaging
Photothermal therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo long-term toxicity of the CDs
In vivo imaging and anti-tumor effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of “Janus” CDs with integrated emission and heat under excitation of NIR light. (b) Absorption spectrum of the CDs. (c) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs. (d) TEM image of the CDs, the inset is high-resolution TEM image. (e) AFM image of the CDs, inset is a height distribution of CDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of the CDs with different concentrations. (b) PLE and PL spectra of the CDs. (c) Lifetime decay curves of the CDs at different wavelengths. (d) 2D top-view TA spectra of the CDs. (e) TA spectra of the CDs at the indicated time from 1.0 to 2000 ps under excitation of 400 nm fs-laser. (f) TA kinetic traces of the CDs were probed at different wavelengths.

Absorption spectra of (a) CDs-2 and CDs-3 dispersed in DMSO. (b) CDs-2 and CDs-3 dispersed in water. The CDs were synthesized by 0.03 mol of citric acid and 0.01 mol of formamide (named as CDs-1), 0.03 mol of citric acid and 0.03 mol of formamide (named as CDs-2), and 0.01 mol of citric acid and 0.03 mol of formamide (named as CDs-3). Time-dependent temperature elevation of the CDs solutions exposed to (c) 808 nm laser and (d) 1064 nm laser. (e) Infrared thermographic images of the CDs solutions under continuous 808 and 1064 nm laser irradiation from 0 to 5 min. (f) Photothermal stability of the CDs in aqueous solution during five cycles (laser on/off) under 1064 nm laser irradiation. (g) Temperature profile of the CDs aqueous solution and pure water irradiated by 1064 nm laser for 10 min, followed by natural cooling. (h) The linear fitting of time from the cooling period versus the negative natural logarithm of driving force temperature for CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra obtained for multi-photon excitation associated with different laser powers of 800 nm femtosecond pulse laser. (b) Fluorescence spectra were obtained with different laser powers of 1200 nm femtosecond pulse laser. (c) Fluorescence spectra were obtained with different laser powers of 1600 nm femtosecond pulse laser. Relationship between the emission intensity and the laser power. (d) 800 nm, (e) 1200 nm, and (f) 1600 nm. (g) Schematic diagram of confocal imaging of cells under multiphoton excitation. The fluorescence confocal images of the cells under excitation of (h) 700 nm and (i) 800 nm. Corresponding cell width measurements under (j) 700 nm excitation, (k) 800 nm excitation.

(a) In vivo imaging of mice at different time points following intravenous injection of the CDs via a tail vein. (b) The images of organs of mice with intravenous injection of the CDs at different times. (c) H&E staining images of heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidney tissues after daily intravenous injection of saline and CDs for 7 days. (dose: 640 μg/mL, 200 μL). (Scale bar = 500 μm).

(a) Treatment schedule on 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. Cell viability of 4T1 cells incubated with CDs at different concentrations under 1064 nm and 808 nm irradiation for (b) 12 h and (c) 24 h incubation. (d) In vivo real-time in vivo imaging of mice with intratumoral injection of CDs at different time points (200 μL, 160 μg/mL). (e) IR thermal images of mice after treatment with CDs (200 μL, 640 μg/mL).at 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 min under irradiation by 808 nm and 1064 nm laser. (f) Relative tumor growth curves of 4T1 tumor mice after therapy. (g) Pictures of tumors were obtained from mice on day 10 after different treatments. (h) The tumor inhibition rate of different treatment groups. (i) H&E morphology evaluation of major organs, scale bar = 100 μm. (j) The TUNEL staining sections from the tumor of mice after photothermal therapy. Data are shown as the mean values ± SD (n = 3); *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared I/II emission and absorption carbon dots via constructing localized excited/charge transfer state for multiphoton imaging and photothermal therapy

Collaborative Innovation Center of New Drug Research and Safety Evaluation

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with absorption and emission properties in near-infrared (NIR) bio-windows highlight their application in the integration of diagnosis and treatment. In this work, CDs with NIR-I emission and NIR-II absorption have been demonstrated, by constructing localized excited state and charge-transfer state. NIR fluorescence together with photothermal effect was observed in the CDs under NIR light illumination. The electron donors (D) and acceptors (A) groups around the carbon core of the CDs lead to D- π -A charge-transfer state, producing a strong electronic push–pull effect and yielding the desired NIR-II absorption with a maximum absorption peak at 1033 nm. Localized excited state from large conjugated rigid sp2 domain leads to NIR-I emission of 670 nm. Multiphoton-induced NIR-I emission (quantum yield is 29%) and NIR-II induced heat with photothermal conversion efficiency of 41.19% have been realized. In addition, multiphoton cellular imaging and cancer photothermal therapy using the CDs as imaging and photothermal agents have been demonstrated. The results provide a route to CDs with NIR absorption/emission characters and prospect their potential application in multiple-photon bioimaging and photothermal therapy of cancers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Near-infrared (NIR) light has been widely employed as an advanced diagnostic and therapeutic tool in the medical field due to its non-invasive control, high resolution, and deep tissue penetration [1,2]. Biocompatible nanoparticles integrated with emission and absorption in NIR bio-windows (NIR-I, 650–950 nm; NIR-II, 1000–1700 nm) have remarkable significance in the field of integrated diagnosis and treatment [3–6]. In particular, carbon dots (CDs) with high bio-compatibility, tunable bandgap, and high emission efficiency, have shown great potential in bioscience and technology field [7–12]. Until now, CDs with red/NIR emission have been achieved [13–15], which promotes their investigation in biological diagnosis and treatment [16–19]. Although the progress of CDs with long-wavelength emission has been made, their absorption spectra peaks were usually located at shorter wavelengths. Extending the absorption and emission of CDs to the NIR-I bio-window and even the NIR-II bio-window is still eagerly wanted. In recent years, CDs with both absorption and emission in the NIR region have been achieved, for example, Lu et al. reported NIR-I emissive CDs with peaks at 665 and 710 nm, which can be excited by 800 nm femtosecond laser [20]. NIR-I CDs with absorption and emission at 710 and 760 nm have been achieved by Qu and co-authors, and 2-photon and 3-photon fluorescence under the excitation of NIR-II light has also been demonstrated [21]. Previously, red/NIR emissive CDs with photoluminescence (PL) quantum yields (QY) of 57% have been synthesized, and 3-photon and 4-photon fluorescence under the excitation of 1200–2000 nm NIR light has been achieved [22]. The longest absorption peaks of the obtained CDs are mostly located in visible and NIR-I regions, while very limited literature on CDs with absorption in the NIR-II region can be found. Liu et al. reported a type of CDs with absorption at around 1070 nm using phenol as a precursor via a one-step solvothermal treatment under a high magnetic field with an intensity of 9 T [23]. Geng et al. increased the absorbance of CDs in the NIR-II bio-window by adjusting the proportion of graphitic nitrogen in the CDs [24]. Tian et al. developed Ni-doped CDs to form a metal–ligand complex structure, enabling them with enhanced NIR-II absorption capacity [25]. However, integrating absorption/emission within a kind of CDs in NIR-II/I bio-windows is still a major challenge. Therefore, achieving effective absorption and emission within a CD will drive CDs towards integrated application of diagnosis and treatment.

Herein, CDs with the longest absorption and emission peak in NIR-II (1033 nm) and NIR-I (670 nm) regions have been demonstrated. The integrated NIR-II absorption and NIR-I emission endow the CDs with excellent photothermal and imaging ability under the excitation of NIR light. Charge transfer between electron donors (D) and acceptors (A) groups bestow NIR-II absorption property of the CDs, and photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of 41.19% can be reached under 1064 nm laser irradiation. In addition, the large conjugated rigid sp2 domain ensures efficient NIR-I emission of the CDs with PL QY of 29%. What’s more, multiphoton-induced fluorescence has been realized under the excitation of NIR light, which has been applied for multiphoton cellular imaging. In addition, the NIR-II absorption of the CDs enables them to efficiently convert NIR light to heat, which can be used for tumor inhibition and ablation.

To synthesize the CDs, a solvent-free carbonization strategy was performed following our previously reported procedure. In brief, citric acid (0.01 mol), formamide (0.03 mol), and 1 g of polyethylene glycol 800 were mixed well in an agate mortar. Then the mixture was transferred to a 50 ml autoclave for heating at 180 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, it was naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting product was added to deionized water and washed twice, and the reactant powder was recovered. Subsequently, the above powder was purified via silica column chromatography using DMSO as the eluent. Finally, the product was freeze-dried to obtain the powder of CDs.

TEM and HRTEM images of the CDs were performed on a JEM-2100 TEM at 200 kV. AFM measurements were performed on a Multimode 8 instrument. SEM was conducted at a JSM-6700F and EDS was performed on Oxford Instruments. Raman spectrum was acquired on Renishaw Raman Spectroscopy System at excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The FTIR spectra were performed with a Thermofisher spectrometer. XPS measurements were conducted using AXIS Supra. The PL and UV–vis-NIR spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000 and Hitachi UH-4150 spectrophotometer, respectively. Fluorescence lifetimes and time-resolution luminescence spectra were measured using FLS1000 time-corrected single photon counting system. The cell imaging pictures were performed at a Leica TCS SP8 laser scanning confocal microscope. The corresponding ultrafast transient absorption spectra were acquired on a sapphire laser (Spectra-Physics, Spitfire ACE, 800 nm). In vivo near-infrared (NIR) images were taken using the IVIS spectrum system (PerkinElmer).

Murine breast cancer cell line 4T1 was obtained from the Key Laboratory of Targeting Therapy and Diagnosis for Critical Diseases in Henan province. Female BALB/c mice were purchased from Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal Co., ltd. All animal procedures were performed in the Institute of Drug Discovery & Development of Zhengzhou University (syxk (yu) 2018–0004) and following the guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Zhengzhou University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo long-term toxicity of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mice were treated with saline and the CDs (dose: 200 μL, 640 μg/mL) via intravenous injection for 7 days. Then the livers and other major organs were harvested for H&E staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging and anti-tumor effect
ce:section.ce:para: Female BALB/c mice were used for in vivo imaging. CDs and saline were injected into the tail vein of mice, respectively. The fluorescence was monitored by an IVIS Spectrum imaging system (PerkinElmer, USA). For the in vivo anti-tumor, when the tumor volume reached to 150 mm3, mice were divided randomly into 4 groups (Saline, CDs, CDs + 1064 nm, CDs + 808 nm) (n = 5). Saline and CDs at a dose of 200 uL were injected via intra-tumoral injection every-two days (5 times in total). Tumor volume and body weight were measured every other day, and the tumor volume was calculated through the formula: (length × width2)/2. After mice were sacrificed, tumors were collected and weighed. The inhibition ratio (IR) was calculated as the following formula: IR (%) = [(Wc-Wt)/Wc] × 100%, where Wc and Wt represented the average tumor weight of control groups and treatment groups, respectively. Moreover, tumors were sectioned and stained for H&E and TUNEL staining.
Data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation by Student’s test or one-way analysis of variance. P values<0.05 were considered statistically significant. (*P < 0.05,**P < 0.01,***P < 0.001).

The CDs in this work were prepared through solvent-free carbonization of citric acid and formamide, and the CDs show emission in NIR-I region and extended absorption peak in NIR-II region. The emission and absorption in NIR-I/II region endow the CDs with excellent bioimaging and photothermal treatment ability in terms of the advantages of NIR lights, and the CDs integrated with emission and heat are illustrated at the bottom of Fig. 1 a. The emission in the NIR-I region origins from the local excited (LE) state involving n-π* transition, while the absorption in the NIR-II region is mainly caused by charge transfer of electron donor and acceptor groups based on the following characterization, as illustrated in the top of Fig. 1a [22]. The absorption spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. 1b, and an obvious absorption peak at around 1033 nm can be observed in addition to the absorption in the NIR-I region. From the PL spectrum of the CDs, NIR-I emission at around 670 nm can be observed (Fig. 1c), and the corresponding PL QY of the CDs is 29% under the excitation of 550 nm. The CDs present uniform morphology from transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) images, as shown in Fig. 1d and 1e. The size of the as-prepared CDs is about 3 nm (Fig. S1), and there is no significant difference compared with previous reports, indicating the observed dimension is not a determining factor for emission [26]. Moreover, well-resolved crystalline lattice fringes of 0.21 nm can be seen from the high-resolution TEM image, indicating the graphite structure of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S2, one broad diffraction peak at 26° can be observed, corresponding to the (002) planes of graphite carbon. And two closely packed peaks at 1580 cm−1 (G band) and 1335 cm−1 (D band) in the Raman spectrum ( Fig. S3 ) represent the G-band (graphitic) and D-band (disordered) of carbon materials [27,28]. Fig. S4 illustrates a typical energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) of the CDs, indicating the main constituents of the CDs are C, N, O and a small amount of S elements come from the dialysate dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). The relative atomic contents were C (51.04%), N (24.95%), O (23.61%) and S (0.39%), respectively. It can be seen that N doping in the CDs is over 20%, and the high level of N content is beneficial to reduce the band gap of the CDs, which is beneficial to the absorption and emission of CDs in the NIR region. The elemental distribution maps indicate that the constituent elements distribute uniformly over the as-prepared CDs. The groups were characterized via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, as shown in Fig. S5. The vibration of CC bonds at 1600 cm−1 indicates the typical aromatic structure of the CDs. The vibration of CO (electron acceptor group) bonds at 1710 cm−1, CN bonds at 1017 cm−1, –NH2 (electron donor group) at 3200 cm−1 and CO-NH (electron donor group) at 1630 cm−1-1690 cm−1 were also observed in the samples [29,30]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) spectra are collected from the dried samples modified with polyethylene glycol, as shown in Fig. S6. The primary elements of the as-prepared CDs are C (285 eV), N (399 eV), and O (531 eV), which are consistent with the FTIR results. Three types of CC/CC (284.5 eV), CN (285.5 eV), and CN/CO (287.1 eV) can be deconvoluted from high-resolution C 1 s spectrum [10]. Two types of N including pyridinic N (399.3 eV) and graphite N (400.7 eV) can be observed from the N1s spectrum [20,31], confirming the electron donor group around the CDs. The O 1 s band contained peaks at 531.6 and 533.1 eV for CO and CO, indicating electron-accepting components around the CDs [32]. These results suggest the formation of aromatic structures and the presence of electron donors and electron acceptor groups, in agreement with the analysis of FTIR results as above. Furthermore, according to previous reports [22], it is believed that D-A or D-π-A structures will be formed by introduced donor and acceptor groups, greatly influencing and improving the optical properties of CDs.

In order to obtain a clear process of carrier transition, recombination, and nonradiative recombination, absorption spectra, PL spectra, and transient absorption (TA) spectra were recorded, and the temperature changes versus illumination time of lights with different wavelengths were also collected. A broad absorption from the visible region to the NIR-II region can be seen from the absorption spectra of the CDs with different concentrations (Fig. 2 a, Fig. S7). The absorption peaks in visible region centered at 580 nm, 620 nm, and 720 nm may origin from the n-π* transitions, which is related to the larger conjugated system containing extensive delocalized electrons in the carbon core. The absorption in the NIR-II region with a peak centered at around 1033 nm is strongly associated with the electron donor and acceptors of the CDs. The blue-purple color solution of the CDs also well confirms the ability of long-wavelength light absorption (Fig. S8). As well as, the stability and dispersion of CDs were investigated. The freeze-dried CDs powders were placed in a drying cabinet for 15 days with a temperature of 25 °C and relative humidity of 40%. The images of the CDs at 1st day and 15th day were taken, as the corresponding absorption spectra of the CDs dispersed in DMSO were collected, as shown in Figs. S9
a and 9b. By comparing the absorption spectra before and after placement, it was found that the main absorption peak positions and shapes of the samples are not significantly changed, which proved the good stability of the CDs. In addition, we checked the stability of CDs in saline and PBS solution. Initially, the CDs in both solutions appeared blue-purple in daylight and the solutions were transparent. Then, these solutions were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator, and images of the solution were taken every 5 days. In Fig. S9
c, no observable discoloration and precipitation was observed after 15 days, exhibiting good stability. Excitation‐emission contour plots of the CDs as excitation light varies from 500 to 700 nm are shown in Fig. S10. The excitation‐independent emission behavior can be seen from excitation-emission spectra, indicating a single luminescence center within the CDs. To test the conclusion, PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the CDs at different wavelengths were monitored. PLE spectra at 630 nm, 670 nm, and 720 nm demonstrate identical peaks (Fig. 2b), indicating the emission comes from radiation recombination of the same excited state [18]. The decay times of the CDs were recorded at different wavelengths, and the decay time of the CDs at 630 nm, 670 nm, and 720 nm is 3.2 ns 3.5 ns, and 3.4 ns, suggesting that the transitions originate from the same excited state (Fig. 2c). To further investigate the decay lifetime of the CDs, the time-resolved fluorescence spectra were measured. The time-resolved spectra of the CDs shown in Fig. S11 indicated an NIR-I emission band at 670 nm with a lifetime of 3 ns, and the shape of PL spectra has no significant change, revealing the time-independent spectral shape of PL emission. To further clarify the internal mechanism of the CDs after excitation, broadband (480–1150 nm) femtosecond transient absorption (TA) experiments were performed under excitation of 400 nm. 2D top view of TA contour plots is shown in Fig. 2d, and a positive and negative signal can be observed from the spectra, respectively. Ground state bleaching (GSB), excited-state absorption (ESA) and stimulated emission (SE) features can be seen from TA spectra [26]. By contrast, only ESA signals are positive signals among these features, and the other two signals are negative. To investigate the detailed process, the TA plots at different times are shown in Fig. 2e. Only one component can be observed from TA spectra at 550 nm, and this is a typical GSB signal combined with absorption spectra of the CDs [33]. The decay curve probed at 655 nm can be fitted by two-component a rapid component and a long component (Fig. 2f). The rapid component corresponds to the population of the lowest excited state, while the long lifetime corresponds to radiative recombination. In addition, a rapid and slow lifetime can be observed in the decay curve probed at 712 nm, which corresponds to excited state absorption and radiative recombination [26]. The transition processes in NIR region are also monitored, and only one rapid process with lifetime can be observed, which corresponds to excited-state absorption. GSB signal is not detected, which may be covered by the strong excited state absorption signal.

To further explore the absorption mechanism of the CDs, the CDs prepared with different molar ratios (citric acid/formamide) of 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3 were analyzed. Obvious absorption peaks in the NIR-II region can be observed in the CDs with precursor ratios of 1:1 (named CDs-2) and 1:3(named CDs-3), as shown in Fig. 3 a. While for the CDs-1 (the ratio of citric acid and formamide is 3:1), only a small shoulder peak was observed (Fig. S12). The absorption peak at 1033 nm becomes more prominent for the CDs-3 with the ratio increases to 1:3, indicating that the formed D- π -A structure of the CDs can enhance their NIR-II absorption. Previous reports also indicate that D- π -A structure can result in a narrow bandgap of CDs [34–36]. Notably, the absorption spectra of the CDs-2 and CDs-3 dispersed in water show obvious absorption in the NIR-II region, as displayed in Fig. 3b. In addition, the CDs-3 dispersed in water maintains a broad absorption in the range of 900–1200 nm showing a slight blue shift due to different polarity and proton-donating-ability of the dispersion solvents [17,21]. The CDs-3 possesses good emission properties in the NIR-I region in addition to NIR-II absorption. In addition, the FTIR results reveal the differences in the surface groups between the three CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S13, the presence of CC (1680 cm−1), CO (1771 cm−1), and –OH bonds indicate CDs-1 were functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups [14]. The CDs-2 (blue line) and CDs-3 (red line) with enhanced vibrational peak at around 3200 cm−1 can be attributed to increase of –NH2. Moreover, the spectrum of CDs-3 shows a broad vibrational signal in range from 1770 cm−1 to 1660 cm−1 compared to the other two CDs. The peaks at 1772 cm−1, 1712 cm−1, 1668 cm−1, 1548 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 bands were attributed to asymmetric CO stretching, symmetric CO stretching, amide I (CO-NH) vibrations, amide II vibrations, and typical CN stretching vibrations, respectively [37]. In particular, the elemental content of the CDs was analyzed by XPS (Fig. S14). The element N contents are 4.02%, 9.87%, and 12.68%, which agrees with FTIR spectra. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS results reveal that amino N has a higher proportion in CDs-2 and CDs-3 compared with CDs-1. The electron-donor (–NH2) and electron-acceptor (-CO) groups on the surface of the CDs form charge-transfer (CT) state and result in absorption in the NIR-II region of the CDs [28,38–40]. Encouraged by the broad absorption in NIR region, we evaluated the photothermal conversion ability of the CDs under the 808 nm (NIR-I light) and 1064 nm (NIR-II light) laser irradiation. The temperature changes of the CDs' aqueous solution (640 μg/mL) versus radiation time are shown in Fig. 3c and 3d. The temperature of the CDs' aqueous solution increases to 39 °C, 56 °C and 79 °C from room temperature under irradiation of 808 nm laser for 600 s at 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 W cm−2, respectively. The temperature of the CDs' aqueous solution increases to 38 °C, 53 °C, and 64.2 °C under excitation of 1064 nm laser with different power densities. The NIR light-induced temperature enhancement was also characterized by an infrared thermal mapping apparatus, and the temperature change can be observed clearly, as shown in Fig. 3e. It is noteworthy that the temperature of the CDs aqueous solution is far beyond 43 °C after irradiation for 300 s under excitation of both 808 nm and 1064 nm laser, which is a temperature threshold that can induce apoptosis [41]. Moreover, there is no significant decrease in photothermal performance of aqueous CDs after 5 cyclic heating/cooling processes, indicating excellent stability (Fig. 3f). The photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of the CDs under the illumination of 1064 nm light with various laser power densities was determined (Fig. 3g and 3 h). Pure water was used as a negative control, the PCE value of CDs was calculated to be as high as 41.19%, according to previous reported strategy [42–44]. Calculation details are given in supporting information (Figs. S15 and S16).

The excellent NIR emission and absorption of the CDs encourage us to investigate the multiphoton excited fluorescence, which has advantages in bioimaging due to high penetration depth and low spontaneous background interference [45]. According to the above analysis, the CDs have a large conjugated rigid sp2 domain surrounded by donor and acceptor groups, it has been reported that CDs with donor-π-acceptor (D-π-A) is in favor of multiphoton fluorescence because of large multiphoton absorption cross-section [46]. In our previous work, a multiphoton-induced fluorescence measurement system has been built, and a long-pass filter was used to filter stray light with high energy [22]. Multiphoton excited fluorescence of the CDs were studied under excitation of 800 nm, 1200 nm, and 1600 nm, and the corresponding multiphoton induced fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 4 a, 4b, and 4c. The fluorescence spectra under excitation of multiphoton and single-photon show the same NIR emission peak, suggesting the radiative recombination originates from an identical excited state. The slopes of emission intensity versus excitation power of 800 nm (NIR-I) and 1200 nm (NIR-II) are 1.98 and 1.83 (Fig. 4d and 4e), which verifies the two-photon induced fluorescence. The identical slope under excitation of 800 nm and 1200 nm indicates broad absorption region. A cubic dependence of emission intensity on excitation fluence is obtained, clearly indicating the three-photon induced emission (Fig. 4f). The multiphoton-induced fluorescence drives us to investigate their application in multiphoton-induced fluorescence bioimaging. In order to study the multiphoton-induced fluorescence bioimaging of the CDs, 4T1 cells are co-incubated with the CDs for 2 h at 37 °C. A schematic diagram of confocal cellular imaging under multiphoton excitation is illustrated in Fig. 4g. The images of the cells under the excitation of 700 nm and 800 nm (two-photon excitation mode) are shown in Fig. 4h and 4i with the corresponding bright field and merge images presented in Fig. S17. A bright, deep red fluorescence can be observed in the cells, and measurement of the fluorescence area allows the approximate length and width of the cells to be deduced (Fig. 4j and 4 k), thus identifying the morphology of the cells. Cell morphology can be observed clearly under excitation of NIR light, verifying the as-prepared CDs can be used as multiphoton bioimaging agents.

In vivo fluorescence images were collected at time points of 0.5, 1, 2, and 24 h, after the intravenous administration of CDs (dose: 160 μg/mL, 200 μL), as shown in Fig. 5 a. In the first 2 h, bright fluorescence can be clearly observed in the whole body of the mice compared with the saline group and the high signal-to-noise ratio of fluorescence was attributed to the superior PL properties of the CDs. Fluorescence signal became very weak after 24 h, indicating CDs can be rapidly excreted from the body of the mice. Fluorescence images of organs including heart, liver, spleen, lungs and kidney were collected at different times. Fig. 5b showed that the fluorescence intensity of these organs decreased with time going by, the CDs mainly accumulated in liver and kidney and only few CDs were found in liver after 24 h. Consequently, these results demonstrate that the intravenously injected CDs rapidly enter the whole body of mice by the blood circulation rather than accumulate at the injection site and then the CDs are excreted via the kidney and liver metabolized system. To further evaluate the long-term in vivo toxicity of the CDs, the mice were treated with saline and the CDs via intravenous injection for 7 days. Then the tissues were sliced and stained by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), and carefully observed under the microscope to analyze pathological changes. From histological analysis results shown in Fig. 5c, there is no symptom of inflammation or lesion in all tissues between the normal saline (control) and experimental groups. Thus, these results indicated that the CDs have good biological compatibility and low toxicity.

The absorption of CDs in NIR-I and NIR-II regions endows them with excellent photothermal properties. Under the excitation of 808 nm (NIR-I) and 1064 nm (NIR-II), NIR photons absorbed by the CDs are converted to heat, and the potency of CDs as a photothermal agent for inhibiting tumor growth was evaluated (Fig. 6 a). Cytotoxicity analysis of the CDs through MTT assay shows that cell survival rate remains above 80 % after incubation with the CDs even at the concentration up to 640 μg/ml for 24 h, suggesting the excellent safety of the CDs (Fig. 6b and 6c). However, dramatic reduction in the number of surviving cells under excitation of 808 nm, less than 25%, is observed after 12 h. The survival rate is only 20% after 24 h, demonstrating the phototoxicity of the CDs. Moreover, the cell viability decreases to 60% when exposed to 1064 nm laser irradiation (CDs concentration at 320 μg/mL). After 1064 nm laser irradiation, the overall survival rate of cells was below 50% after incubation with CDs at a concentration of 640 μg/mL for 24 h. In order to determine the residence time of the CDs in tumor, 200 μL of CDs solution was injected intratumorally. A strong fluorescent signal was detected by a multi-mode living imager under excitation of 570 nm light, owing to the high fluorescence efficiency of the CDs. The NIR-I emission of the CDs provides a solid tool for monitoring the content of the CDs in tumor. The fluorescent signal in the tumor region could still be easily detected and observed 24 h after injection, indicating that CDs can stay in the tumor for a long time (Fig. 6d). Ex vivo images of mice with the injection of the CDs after 24 h are shown in Fig. S18. To evaluate in vivo photo-induced heat by the CDs, the temperature change in tumor region after laser irradiation was recorded by an infrared thermal mapping apparatus. As shown in Fig. 6e, only mild temperature increase was detected in the mice from saline group, and the final temperature maintains at 43 °C. In contrast, the temperature of the tumor region rapidly elevated up to 52 °C under either 808 nm or 1064 nm laser irradiation after CDs injection, which are sufficient to cause irreversible damage to the tumor tissue [41,47,48], indicating that the CDs can be used as tumoricidal photothermal agent. The photothermal therapy (PTT) efficacy of the CDs was further evaluated by monitoring the tumor growth rate (Fig. 6f). The tumor volume in saline and the CDs groups without laser irradiation increased quickly. In comparison, the tumor growth rate significantly slow down under 808 and 1064 nm laser irradiation, which strongly indicate that the CDs can convert NIR light to localized heat and further inhibit the tumor proliferation. Pictures of tumors were obtained from mice on day 10, as displayed in Fig. 6g. More than 50% and 60% tumor growth inhibition were achieved with CDs plus 1064 nm and 808 nm treatment (Fig. 6h), respectively. Slightly higher antitumor efficacy was observed in CDs activated at 808 nm group, due to the higher absorption coefficient of CDs in the NIR-I region compared to the NIR-II region. To better verify the therapeutic effect and biosafety of the CDs that are used for PTT, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining sections from the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidney, and tumor) of mice were examined (Fig. 6i). It is found that maximum dead cells, bulk necrosis, and acellular regions appeared in tumor tissues after treatment with CDs under laser irradiation. At the same time, no abnormal signs of organ damage or inflammatory lesions were observed in PTT treated group (Fig. S19), indicating the low systemic toxicity [49,50]. Intratumoral apoptosis of the tumor sections was detected via a TUNEL assay after photothermal treatment (Fig. 6j, Fig. S20). Large areas of apoptosis were found in CDs + 1064 nm and CDs + 808 nm groups. The apoptosis was caused by PTT efficacy of CDs under NIR-I/II irradiation, which is also consistent with the tumor growth curves. Fig. S21 shows body weight change of mice, and there was no obvious weight loss in the mice, suggesting no significant side effect. These results indicate that the novel emission and heat integrated CDs could be used as photothermal and photoluminescence agents, which was promising for anticancer therapy.

In summary, CDs with expanded NIR-II absorption together with NIR-I emission have been prepared, and the CDs show both photoluminescent and photothermal properties. The localized excited state accounts for the efficient NIR-I (670 nm) emission, and the charge transfer state results in NIR-II (1033 nm) absorption. Multiphoton-induced NIR-I emission of the CDs has been investigated and applied for cellular and tumor imaging. The heat generated by the CDs under excitation of NIR-I/II light enables their treatment ability of tumor. This novel photoluminescence and photothermal integrated CDs shown in this work have potential in the field of cancer diagnostics and treatment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11904326 and 62075198), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019TQ0287, 2019M662510). Henan Center for Outstanding Overseas Scientists (GZS201903).
